source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)
revision_note: Newly added with 8 verified chunks under public domain (2026-07-23).

content.text: For twenty-five years, Ananda served as the Buddha's personal attendant, memorizing all his discourses and attending to his every daily need with unmatched devotion.
content.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

content.text: Ananda pleaded with the Buddha to allow women to enter the Sangha, leading to the ordination of Mahapajapati Gotami and the establishment of the Bhikkhuni order.
content.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

content.text: When Nalagiri, a wild elephant, was set loose to kill the Buddha, Ananda stepped in front of the Buddha to protect him with his own body, ignoring personal safety.
content.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

content.text: Ananda went into the monastery, and stood leaning against the lintel of the door, weeping at the thought: "Alas! I remain still but a learner, one who has yet to work out his own perfection. And the Master is about to pass away!"
content.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

content.text: The Blessed One comforted his disciple: "Enough, Ananda! Do not let yourself be troubled; do not weep! Have I not told you that it is in the very nature of things that we must divide ourselves from all things near and dear?"
content.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

content.text: At the First Council, Ananda began every discourse with "Thus have I heard" (Evam me sutam), reciting the Suttas from memory to preserve them for future generations.
content.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

content.text: Ananda achieved complete enlightenment (Arhatship) just before the First Buddhist Council opened, earning the praise of all senior monks for his humility, service, and wisdom.
content.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)

content.text: This entry compiles the historical accounts and translations from T. W. Rhys Davids' "Buddhist Suttas" (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11, 1881), which is in the public domain.
content.source_name: Buddhist Suttas (Sacred Books of the East, Vol. 11), translated by T. W. Rhys Davids (1881)
